text:
KINGS NEUROSCIENCES CENTRE


Clinic Date: 02 October 2025

Dr Kate
Health Centre

Keele University, Keele, Newcastle

 Staffordshire ST5 5BG
Dear Dr Kate
Wendy Brown, DOB: 21-11-1982, Hospital No: K482715 NHS No. 6592841037
Flat 7 Brookside Avenue, Newcastle-under-Lyme, ST5 2QD 

Diagnoses/summary: Deteriorating focal epilepsy, likely temporal lobe onset, with suspected catamenial exacerbation. Prior EEG demonstrated left temporal intermittent rhythmic delta activity; prior MRI (2018) structurally normal, no clear epileptogenic lesion.

Medication history: Previously tried and discontinued due to side effects or insufficient efficacy: lamotrigine (skin rash at higher doses), levetiracetam (mood lability), carbamazepine (dizziness), lacosamide (diplopia), clobazam PRN (sedation), valproate (avoided for teratogenic considerations, briefly trialled historically), topiramate (word-finding difficulty). Current medication: brivaracetam 100 mg twice daily; zonisamide 200 mg nocte; folic acid 5 mg daily; vitamin D3 1000 IU daily. No known drug allergies.

Interim history: I reviewed Wendy Brown in clinic today; she attended alone. Over the past six months she reports a clear deterioration in seizure control with cyclical perimenstrual clustering, consistent with a catamenial pattern. She notes increased premonitory déjà vu and rising epigastric sensations on days -3 to +3 around menses, with superimposed clusters in the evenings. Generalised tonic-clonic seizures are rare, typically four events per year. focal impaired awareness seizures occur several times each week, particularly in the evenings. She keeps a seizure diary that demonstrates temporal correlation with her menstrual cycle. Sleep disruption and stress at work appear to lower the threshold further. No recent head injuries. No status epilepticus. Post-ictal confusion is prolonged compared to baseline, often 30–45 minutes, which marks a deterioration from last year.

Safety/impact: Two recent near-falls in the bathroom; no injuries. She does not drive and is aware of DVLA regulations. Employer has arranged flexible hours; she remains anxious about performance and fatigue. No current pregnancy plans; uses barrier contraception.

Examination: Neurological examination today was unremarkable. Weight stable, BMI 24. Blood pressure 118/72. No focal deficits. Mood mildly anxious but not depressed by screening questions.

Investigations: Routine bloods (March 2025) normal including U&Es, LFTs, bicarbonate 25 mmol/L. Zonisamide level within expected therapeutic range (laboratory reference). MRI brain with epilepsy protocol (2018) normal. I have requested repeat 3T MRI with hippocampal volumetry and outpatient video-EEG telemetry to characterise evening events and assess for lateralising features.

Plan:
- Seizure management: Given clear perimenstrual clustering, we discussed catamenial epilepsy strategies. Options included short-cycle benzodiazepine bridge (clobazam) around menses, acetazolamide perimenstrually, or hormonal modulation. She prefers to avoid daily sedation. We agreed a trial of clobazam 10 mg nocte starting three days before expected menses and continuing to day +3, with the option to increase to 10 mg twice daily if tolerability permits. I provided a written plan and discussed risks of sedation and dependence with intermittent short-course use.
- Background anti-seizure therapy: Continue brivaracetam 100 mg twice daily and zonisamide 200 mg nocte for now. If inadequate response after two cycles, consider switch of backbone therapy to cenobamate (slow titration) or revisit lamotrigine via slow desensitisation pathway if benefits outweigh prior rash risk—this will be considered only after allergy/immunology advice.
- Hormonal considerations: I have asked our neuro-endocrine clinic to advise on cycle regulation options (e.g. natural progesterone in luteal phase) given the suspected catamenial pattern. Patient understands that combined hormonal contraception may interact with certain anti-seizure medicines; current regimen has minimal interaction risk.
- Safety: Reinforced SUDEP risk mitigation, nocturnal supervision strategies, and seizure first-aid. Provided updated rescue plan with buccal midazolam 10 mg for seizures >5 minutes or recurrent without recovery; community nurses to review training.
- Monitoring and support: Arrange nurse specialist telephone follow-up in 4 weeks to review perimenstrual clobazam response, sedation, and seizure diary. Clinic review in 3 months or sooner if escalation occurs. I have provided a work support letter regarding flexible scheduling and rest breaks.

I emphasised that the current pattern represents a deterioration and warrants close monitoring while we implement the above adjustments and complete updated investigations. Please continue current medications and perimenstrual clobazam as outlined; I will update you after telemetry and MRI.

Yours sincerely

entities.text: focal impaired awareness seizures occur several times each week
Generalised tonic-clonic seizures are rare, typically four events per year. focal impaired awareness seizures occur several times each week, particularly in the evenings.

check__Seizure Frequency Number.analysis: Identify explicit seizure frequency statements. The letter states: 'Generalised tonic-clonic seizures are rare, typically four events per year.' and 'focal impaired awareness seizures occur several times each week, particularly in the evenings.' Apply the rule to select the highest frequency among seizure types; 'several times each week' is more frequent than 'four per year'. 'Several' is vague; normalize as 'multiple per week' per allowed format using 'multiple' when exact number cannot be determined. No other more frequent numeric frequency is provided. Therefore, final label is 'multiple per week'.
seizure_frequency_number: focal impaired awareness seizures occur several times each week
reference: Generalised tonic-clonic seizures are rare, typically four events per year. focal impaired awareness seizures occur several times each week, particularly in the evenings.
